title: About
text:
This site is the public write-up of my Google Summer of Code (GSoC) 2026 work on gfpop. It does two things:
Click Home in the top navigation to read the posts.

title: About
text:
What is gfpop?
gfpop = Graph-constrained Functional Pruning Optimal Partitioning — an R package with a C++ core for multiple change-point detection under graph constraints, by Vincent Runge (LaMME, Université d’Évry).
You detect change-points in a 1-D signal, but constrain how the segment means may change by defining a graph of states and edges. That single dynamic-programming engine then expresses isotonic regression, up/down patterns, exponential decay, robust fits, a fixed number of segments, and more.

Upstream repo: https://github.com/vrunge/gfpop
Method paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/2002.03646
Install: devtools::install_github("vrunge/gfpop")

title: About
text:
About me
Maintained by williamzhang7792. I contribute to the upstream gfpop package via the usual fork-and-PR flow; this repo is the standalone public record of that work.



My first GSoC meeting, May 2026: an early video call with my mentors, at the start of the time-dependent constraints project.

title: Adding a rule column to gfpop: backward compatible by default
text: PR B is the second in the midterm series and the first one that touches the API. It adds a rule column to gfpop’s graph, the column that later PRs will use to switch edges on and off along the signal. On its own it does nothing yet, by design.

title: Adding a rule column to gfpop: backward compatible by default
text:
What the column is
A gfpop graph is a data frame of edges. Each row is one allowed transition, carrying its type, penalty, and parameters. PR B gives each row one more field: rule, an integer id, defaulting to NA.
NA means the edge is active under every rule. A graph built the old way, with no rule set, has NA on every edge and behaves exactly as it did before. The column is recorded and ignored until PR D teaches the solver to read it.
It is added wherever a graph is built. Edge() takes a new rule = argument, and graph(), StartEnd(), and Node() all carry the field, so the data frame has the same shape no matter how you assemble it.
# default rule = NA: active in every rule, identical to before
Edge("bkg", "bkg", type = "null")

# explicit: this edge is active only under rule 2
Edge("bkg", "peak", type = "up", penalty = 10, rule = 2)

title: Adding a rule column to gfpop: backward compatible by default
section: Why it goes upstream first, as a draft
text:
Why it goes upstream first, as a draft
The rule column is the API surface everything else is built on. Once PRs C and D depend on its shape, changing it is expensive. So it goes upstream on its own, as a draft: #21. The draft is an invitation. Before I stack three more PRs on this shape, I want Vincent’s read on whether a per-edge integer id is the right way to express a rule, while it is still cheap to change.

title: Adding a rule column to gfpop: backward compatible by default
text:
Backward compatibility
The NA default exists so that nothing changes for existing code, and that is a claim worth a test rather than a promise. PR B adds tests/testthat/test-rule.R: twenty-two tests covering the default and explicit rule on Edge(), the validation errors for a bad value, that StartEnd() and Node() carry the same column as Edge(), that reordering a graph preserves its rule values, and a full gfpop() solve that returns the same result with the column present as without it.
One test paid for itself right away. I first declared the empty graph skeletons with numeric(0), a double. When graphReorder() rebuilds a graph by row-binding, a double rule column stays double, and the test that checks the reordered values came back “equal but not identical”: 1 where it wanted 1L. The fix was to declare the skeleton column as integer(0), so the rule stays a clean integer from the R side through to the integer vector the C++ will read in PR D. Small, but the test caught it before it could turn into a confusing type mismatch later.

title: Adding a rule column to gfpop: backward compatible by default
text:
A small blast radius
graphReorder() needed no new logic. It indexes its columns by position, so the rule column travels along with the rest untouched. PR B adds a field and changes no behavior, which is what makes it safe to put in front of the maintainer first.

title: Adding a rule column to gfpop: backward compatible by default
text:
Related PRs

PR #21, open as a draft so the rule API can be discussed before later PRs build on it.

title: Adding CI to gfpop: the first neutral reader
text:
The midterm work on gfpop is five small pull requests, A through E, mostly independent, building toward a LOPART oracle at the end. PR A was supposed to be the “dull” one: add a continuous-integration workflow so that everything after it gets checked on every push, and move on.
But still, I faced one challenge that I had to fix.

title: Adding CI to gfpop: the first neutral reader
text:
What CI actually is
Turning CI on is the first time the package runs somewhere that is not my laptop. A fresh Ubuntu, macOS, and Windows runner, R release, nothing installed that I forgot I had installed two years ago. CI is the first neutral reader of your code. It has none of your context, and so it tells you, plainly, what your own machine has been quietly hiding.
What it told me here was that gfpop could not go green by configuration alone. The package source had to change.

title: Adding CI to gfpop: the first neutral reader
text:
The workflow
The file is the standard r-lib R-CMD-check, dropped at .github/workflows/R-CMD-check.yaml. A three-OS matrix on R release, and triggers that fire on a push to any branch and on pull requests into main or master:
on:
  push:
  pull_request:
    branches: [main, master]
The one part that matters for the rest of this story is dependency setup. CI preinstalls what the check needs before it runs:
- uses: r-lib/actions/setup-r-dependencies@v2
  with:
    extra-packages: |
      any::rcmdcheck
      any::remotes
      github::vrunge/gfpop.data
    needs: check
Note the last line. gfpop.data is GitHub-only, not on CRAN, so CI installs it from GitHub with the github:: prefix. That detail is the whole knot, so hold onto it.
One choice worth a sentence: the push trigger is a bare push:, which runs on every branch, not just main and master. That is convenient for getting a green check before a pull request exists. It also spends the maintainer’s Actions minutes on every branch push once this is upstream, so the PR offers to restrict it if Vincent would rather.

title: Adding CI to gfpop: the first neutral reader
text:
What the neutral reader caught
The first run failed, and not where I expected. Two places in the package were installing a dependency over the network at the wrong moment.
The test suite did it. tests/testthat/test-sn.R opened with library(devtools) and a call to devtools::install_github("vrunge/gfpop.data"), at test time. One vignette, vignettes/applications.Rmd, did the same thing at vignette-build time. On my machine both were invisible: I already had gfpop.data, the install was a no-op, the tests passed. In the clean CI sandbox they failed with:
The package "remotes" is required.
devtools::install_github delegates to remotes, and the isolated subprocess that R CMD check spins up to run the tests could not see it.
That last point is the real lesson. Adding remotes to extra-packages does not fix it (it is already there): a network install inside R CMD check is in the wrong place to begin with. The check runs in its own sandbox, and a build that reaches out to GitHub mid-check is fragile no matter what the workflow preinstalls. So the fix belongs in the package, not the workflow.

title: Adding CI to gfpop: the first neutral reader
text:
The fix
So the infrastructure-only PR grew a second half: make the suite CI-friendly.
In test-sn.R, the runtime install came out and a dependency check went in. The file asks whether its dependencies are present, then each test skips cleanly if they are not:
have.sn.deps <- requireNamespace("gfpop.data", quietly = TRUE) &&
  requireNamespace("data.table", quietly = TRUE)
test_that("abs model with 1 segment returned", {
  skip_if_not(have.sn.deps, "gfpop.data/data.table not installed")
  ...
})
Because gfpop.data is preinstalled in CI through extra-packages, have.sn.deps is true on the runners and the sn tests run for real. On a contributor’s machine without the data package, they skip with a clear message instead of trying to reach the network mid-check. The tests are honest about what they need, and they ask the environment instead of reshaping it.
applications.Rmd got the same treatment: the build-time install removed, the vignette now leaning on the preinstalled gfpop.data, with a comment showing how to install it by hand. Two small housekeeping items rounded it out, a ^\.github$ line in .Rbuildignore so the workflow does not get bundled into the package tarball, and an R-CMD-check badge on the README.
The result is the thing CI exists to produce. R CMD check reports Status: OK, zero errors, zero warnings, zero notes, on all three operating systems, and the vignettes rebuild without touching the network.
A green check gives me a neutral reader that runs on three machines that are not mine and flags a break the moment it happens, before anyone else has to find out. It does not make the code correct, but it is the floor a project about to add five PRs of new behavior stands on. It is why I started with CI.

title: Adding CI to gfpop: the first neutral reader
text:
Related PRs

PR #20

title: Starting GSoC 2026: the project and how this blog works
text:
This blog tracks my Google Summer of Code 2026 work on gfpop, an R package with a C++ core that detects change-points in a signal under graph constraints. The idea is to describe the allowed shape of a segmentation as a graph of states and edges, and let one dynamic-programming engine fit isotonic, up-down, and other patterns through that single interface. One engine, many shapes.



Change-point detection on a one-dimensional signal: noisy data, a piecewise-constant fit, and the change-points gfpop recovers.


My project is Time-Dependent Constraints in gfpop. Today that graph is fixed in time: the same edges apply at every data point. That rules out any model whose allowed transitions change along the signal. LOPART is the example I keep coming back to, where what is permitted depends on whether a point falls inside a labeled region. The project adds a rule function that picks which edges are active at each time step, so the constraint graph can vary across the data instead of standing still.
The concrete work is a rule= argument on Edge() and gfpop(), the C++ edge filtering that backs it, and two validation models: LOPART, checked against the standalone LOPART package, and an up-down-with-labels model on synthetic genomic data. Two constraints sit underneath all of it. The argument stays backward compatible, and regression tests prove gfpop without rule returns exactly what it does today, down to the value. A capstone vignette ties it together.
The full write-up and timeline are in the proposal: Time-Dependent Constraints in gfpop.

title: Starting GSoC 2026: the project and how this blog works
text:

title: Starting GSoC 2026: the project and how this blog works
text:
How this blog is structured
The blog has two jobs.
The first is a contribution log. Each time I change something in gfpop upstream, a post here explains what changed and why, and links the pull request it came from. Those posts close with a short Related PRs section listing the upstream PR as a plain link like [PR #123](https://github.com/vrunge/gfpop/pull/123), so the code and the write-up always point back at each other.
The second is tutorials: posts that show how to use gfpop, with runnable R. The site is built with Quarto, which runs the code in a post at render time. So the output and plots you see are produced by gfpop, not pasted in by hand.
Each post lives in its own folder under posts/. There are no upstream PRs yet, so this first post has no Related PRs section.
Every contribution post after it will.

title: How to hand a maintainer a whole feature: the midterm in five PRs
text:
The midterm deliverable for my GSoC project is the core feature: it makes the constraint graph vary along the signal, so which edges are allowed depends on where you are in the data. The question here is narrower and more practical: once you have built something that size, how do you hand it to a maintainer?
Not as one diff. A single pull request that adds an R argument, threads it through the C++ solver, changes the dynamic-programming loops, and lands a new validated model is a request the reviewer cannot actually review. They can skim it, trust it, and merge it, or they can refuse it. Neither is what you want. What you want is for the maintainer to read each piece, agree with it on its own terms, and merge it while the rest is still on your fork.
So the work is five pull requests, A through E. Each one is small enough to read in a sitting, backward compatible by construction, and mergeable on its own. The trunk stays green from the first to the last. The opener is deliberately boring. The payoff at the end is an oracle test.

title: How to hand a maintainer a whole feature: the midterm in five PRs
text:
The five PRs
PR A, CI. A GitHub Actions R-CMD-check workflow across Ubuntu, macOS, and Windows. This is the dull one on purpose, the foundation everything else stands on: a neutral reader that checks the package on three machines that are not mine, on every push. It earned its own write-up, the first neutral reader, because turning it on surfaced a network install hiding inside the test suite and the fix was in the package, not the YAML. Fully independent of the rest. Open upstream as #20.
PR B, the R rule API. This adds a backward-compatible rule column to Edge(), and carries it through graph(), StartEnd(), and Node(). The default is NA, which means an edge is active in every rule, so a graph written today behaves exactly as it did before. Pure R, and no behavior change yet: the column is recorded and ignored. It is its own PR because the API shape is the part most worth arguing about before any code depends on it, so it goes up as a draft, #21, to invite that argument early.
PR C, the inert C++ plumbing. This adds the ruleID field to the C++ edge representation, a getter, a Graph::isActive() helper, and a defensive read of the new rule column out of the R graph. Nothing consumes any of it yet, so every result is bit-for-bit what it was before. It is backward compatible by construction, and an inert-equality test proves it. Splitting the plumbing from the activation means the reviewer can confirm the new machinery is genuinely dormant before they look at the switch that turns it on. Independent of B in principle, stacked on it in practice, for a reason I will get to.
PR D, activation. This is the behavioral switch. It threads a per-data-point rule vector through gfpop() and into the solver, and skips inactive edges inside the dynamic-programming loops. Before D, the rule column is a passenger; after D, it steers. It depends on B for the API and C for the C++ scaffolding. This is also where the work was expected to be hard, and the honest note below is about why it was not.
PR E, the LOPART oracle. The midterm payoff. It builds the four-rule, two-state graph that expresses LOPART, constructs the rule vector from a set of labels, and then asserts that gfpop with the rule argument reproduces LOPART::LOPART() exactly: the same change-points, the same segment means, the same loss. It depends on D, because it is the first thing that runs the whole chain end to end and checks the answer against an independent implementation.

title: How to hand a maintainer a whole feature: the midterm in five PRs
section: How they depend on each other
text:
How they depend on each other
A, B, and C are independent. Each one can be built straight off master without the other two. D needs B and C. E needs D.



The midterm as five pull requests: PR A stands alone, while PRs B and C converge on D, which leads to E.


The true shape is a diamond, but in practice I built it as a straight line, A then B then C then D then E. The reason is small and concrete. C’s inert-equality test is cleaner when it can use B’s new Edge(rule = ...) API to construct its test graph, rather than reaching around it. Stacking C on B costs nothing, since D needs both anyway, and a linear stack is far simpler to keep green than a diamond you have to merge back together. When the difference between the honest dependency graph and the build order is one convenience edge, take the simpler build order.

title: How to hand a maintainer a whole feature: the midterm in five PRs
text:
What’s hard
PR D was supposed to be the hard one. The proposal called out infinity handling in the constraint operators as the risk: when an edge is switched off, the cost of taking it has to become infinite in a way the operators downstream handle without breaking, and I expected to be editing the min-cost operators one by one to make that true.
I did not have to touch them. The solver already initializes unreachable states to positive infinity, and the whole functional-pruning machinery is already built to propagate that correctly. Skipping an inactive edge is, as far as the operators are concerned, the same as never having had a path to it, which is a case they have always handled. The part I had budgeted the most worry for turned out to be free. That happens sometimes, and it is worth saying plainly rather than dressing it up as foresight.

title: How to hand a maintainer a whole feature: the midterm in five PRs
section: Why A through E is the midterm
text:
Why A through E is the midterm
The slicing is not arbitrary. A through E is exactly the set of “core goals, must deliver” from my proposal, which is what makes it a natural milestone rather than a place I stopped because the clock ran out.
The mapping is one to one:

Edge(..., rule = ...), backward compatible: PR B.
gfpop(..., rule = ...), backward compatible: PR D.
C++ edge filtering by rule: PR D.
Infinity handling in the constraint operators: PR D, satisfied by reusing the existing machinery (the surprise above).
A LOPART model, oracle-validated against LOPART::LOPART(): PR E.
Regression tests, gfpop with no rule identical to current behavior: PR B, C, and D each carry their own.
R CMD check passes with no new warnings: PR A, and held by all of them.

Every must-deliver box is checked. The stretch goals are deliberately Part 2: the up-down-with-labels model on synthetic genomic data, the performance benchmarks from a thousand points up to a million, and the capstone vignette with a filmstrip view of the graph changing along the signal. Those are real and they are next; they are just not what the midterm is for.

title: How to hand a maintainer a whole feature: the midterm in five PRs
text:
Where it stands
A and B are open upstream: #20 is the CI workflow, and #21 is the rule API, sitting as a draft so the shape can be discussed before C, D, and E lean on it. C, D, and E are staged on my fork and will become their own PRs once the API in B has settled, since opening them now would only ask the maintainer to review against a moving target.
The whole stack is assembled and green on a prototype branch, the LOPART oracle included. That branch is the proof that the diamond closes. The PRs are how I hand it over one honest piece at a time.

title: How to hand a maintainer a whole feature: the midterm in five PRs
text:
Related PRs
This post is the hub for the series; each PR gets its own write-up as it goes upstream.

PR A, CI: PR #20. Write-up: the first neutral reader.
PR B, the R rule API: PR #21, open as a draft.
PR C, inert C++ plumbing: staged on the prototype branch, PR to follow once B settles.
PR D, activation: staged on the prototype branch, PR to follow.
PR E, the LOPART oracle: staged on the prototype branch, PR to follow.
